verses.text: Saul takes three thousand chosen men to hunt David and, by God's strange providence, steps into the very cave where David and his men are hiding. The hunter walks unknowing into the hands of the hunted. God can deliver an enemy to us — and so test what is in our heart.

Saul takes three thousand chosen men to hunt David and, by God's strange providence, steps into the very cave where David and his men are hiding. The hunter walks unknowing into the hands of the hunted. God can deliver an enemy to us — and so test what is in our heart.

Saul takes three thousand chosen men to hunt David and, by God's strange providence, steps into the very cave where David and his men are hiding. The hunter walks unknowing into the hands of the hunted. God can deliver an enemy to us — and so test what is in our heart.

David's men urge him: here is your chance, kill him! But after cutting off a corner of Saul's robe, David's heart smites him. He will not lift his hand against 'the LORD's anointed.' True faith refuses to seize by sin what God has promised to give in His own time.

David's men urge him: here is your chance, kill him! But after cutting off a corner of Saul's robe, David's heart smites him. He will not lift his hand against 'the LORD's anointed.' True faith refuses to seize by sin what God has promised to give in His own time.

David's men urge him: here is your chance, kill him! But after cutting off a corner of Saul's robe, David's heart smites him. He will not lift his hand against 'the LORD's anointed.' True faith refuses to seize by sin what God has promised to give in His own time.

David comes out and calls after Saul, bowing low: 'the LORD delivered thee into mine hand... but I spared thee.' He proves his innocence by the very mercy he showed. The best answer to those who hate us is not revenge but undeniable, returned good.

David comes out and calls after Saul, bowing low: 'the LORD delivered thee into mine hand... but I spared thee.' He proves his innocence by the very mercy he showed. The best answer to those who hate us is not revenge but undeniable, returned good.

Holding up the piece of robe, David pleads, 'there is neither evil nor transgression in mine hand,' and leaves the verdict with God: 'the LORD judge between me and thee.' He will defend himself with words and trust, but he will not avenge himself by force.

Holding up the piece of robe, David pleads, 'there is neither evil nor transgression in mine hand,' and leaves the verdict with God: 'the LORD judge between me and thee.' He will defend himself with words and trust, but he will not avenge himself by force.

David calls himself 'a dead dog, a flea' — is this whom a king pursues? He humbles himself utterly and commits his cause to God as judge. The way up, in God's kingdom, runs through the low ground of humility and trust, not self-assertion.

David calls himself 'a dead dog, a flea' — is this whom a king pursues? He humbles himself utterly and commits his cause to God as judge. The way up, in God's kingdom, runs through the low ground of humility and trust, not self-assertion.

Saul weeps and confesses, 'thou art more righteous than I... thou hast rewarded me good, whereas I have rewarded thee evil.' Even a hardened enemy is, for a moment, melted by undeserved kindness. Returned good can shame a foe as no retaliation ever could.

Saul admits David will surely be king and begs him to spare his descendants. David swears it, and they part. David, who could have ended it all in the cave, leaves the throne to God's timing — and keeps faith even with the house of the man who hunts him.
